title: Week 1 Lecture
body:
Acknowledgement of Country

I acknowledge the Bedegal people that are the Traditional Custodians of this land. I would also like to pay my respects to the Elders both past and present and extend that respect to other Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islanders who are present here today.

SOCIAL ENTREPRENEURSHIP PRACTICUM (COMM3030)

LECTURE – WEEK 1 Topic: Introduction to Social Impact and Social Enterprise

Reminder: O-Week Lecture If you haven't already, please go to Moodle and review the content in the O-Week section before this lecture.

It will give you an overview of the course (inc. course format, assessments, schedule and Moodle run through)

Thank you!

Assessments Assessment task Weighting Due Date and Time

Assessment 1: Individual Research 40% Monday 19th January by 11am (Week 3) Video

8-minute video & PPT Slides

Assessment 2: Project Deliverables 30% Wednesday 4th February by 11am (Week 6)

3,000-word group report

Assessment 3: Participation & Peer 30% Participation – Ongoing Evaluation Peer Evaluation – Friday 6th February by 5pm (Week 6)

Assessment 2 should read 3500-words

The complete 'Assessment Details' document i s available in Assessment Hub on Moodie

Key Dates and Information for Assessments

1. Monday 19th January by 11am (Week 3) Individual Research Video 2. Wednesday 4th February by 11am (Week 5) Group Report 3. Peer Evaluation – Friday 6th February by 5pm Individual Peer Evaluation Participation – Ongoing

All tasks in class are linked stages that culminate in your Group Report Submission times cannot be extended. Late penalties will apply even if only 1 minute late Academic Integrity will be rigidly assessed, and submissions of concern will be referred to authorities for further action

Using generative AI (GenAI) responsibly Comm3030 allows ethical use of GenAI but has strict guidelines to ensure academic integrity. What you can & can’t do Allowed: Using AI for idea generation, research assistance, or proofreading as specified by each assignment’s guidelines. Not allowed: *AI rewriting your work by changing your original text’s wording, structure or phrasing. (this includes tools like Grammarly/Grammarly Go). *Full translations using a translator or other AI. Use your own words and understanding. Misuse consequences: violating GenAI rules may lead to reports to the Conduct and Integrity Office.

Acknowledge AI Use If you use AI, you must reference it according to the UNSW guide for referencing AI use.

What is social impact?

“Social impact can be defined as the net effect of an activity on a community and the well-being of individuals and families.” Centre for Social Impact

Important: the net effect can be intended or unintended, positive or negative. Social impact is not always good.

Where does social impact come from? Traditionally, social impact has been generated by three main sectors:

• Government • Business • Non-Profits

Increasingly individuals are regarded as having the capacity to create social impact and could be considered as a fourth sector.

In addition, there is a new and emerging fifth sector that has evolved as a hybrid of other sectors.

Who creates social impact? Government Non-profits Business Individuals

• Social Impact • Programs and • Products and • Voting Framework Services Services • Philanthropy and • Social Policy • Research and • Corporate Social Participation • Leadership & Advocacy Responsibility • Consumer choice Coordination e.g. • Creating Shared UN SDGs Value

What is Social Enterprise?

operates as a not-for-profit organisation, seeking to generate a surplus for the purpose of reinvesting; has a defined primary social purpose, environmental or other public benefit; derives a substantial portion of its income from trade; and is a legally compliant enterprise including meeting all relevant legislative requirements.

The Social Impact Project

What is it?

An immersive, hands-on learning experience Learn about social enterprise and business planning Develop a concept for a Surf Lifesaving Sydney Receive exclusive resources and feedback from Surf Lifesaving Sydney Pitch your idea

The Social Impact Project

Benefits of participation

Gain a deeper understanding of social enterprise and behind-the-scenes insights Develop valuable employability skills and experience Receive personalised feedback on your idea Explore career pathways in social impact

Surf Life Saving Sydney

Briefing by Gary Driscoll, Deputy President, Surf Lifesaving Sydney

we are focusing on providing SLS with suggestions for leveraging their brand and increasing revenue streams.

Project Management

This will be covered in your tutorial and will lead to you completing the Project Scope Template.

Project Scope Template • Downloadable in Assessment Hub. • This is to encourage you to reach a shared understanding of your client, your objectives and your deliverables early on. Sections 2 and 3 must be completed individually.

See You Next Week!
